title: Test coverage discipline — scope what you test, not what you implemented this turn
when_to_read: Before declaring a `build`-phase task done, before calling `assess_spec({mode:'phase', target:'verify'})`, and any time `list_acs` shows ACs at 0 tests. Names the lazy patterns to watch for in your own work.
body:
Implementation ACs commit your codebase to specific empirical claims. An AC with no tagged test in the codebase is a silent gap — Memex can't tell whether the code honours the claim. The number on `list_acs` is the truth: ACs at 0 tests are unfinished work, regardless of whether the production code 'looks right' or whether you've moved on to other tasks.

Three patterns to recognise (and reject) in your own work:

## Pattern 1: scoping tests to 'just this turn's task'

You pick up a Spec in `build` with 10 active ACs. You're tasked with implementing the production code for ac-1 and ac-5. You write tagged tests for those two. You declare the task done.

**This is wrong.** The TDD discipline is to write tagged tests for every AC the Spec commits to, not just the ones you happen to be implementing this turn. The other 8 ACs need RED tests now — they'll go GREEN as their owning tasks land. Without them, Memex has no way to know whether the Spec is being honoured, and the dashboard sits at low coverage even when the code is making real progress.

The correct move on this kind of turn:
  1. Write tagged tests (failing RED) for **every** active AC on the Spec.
  2. Implement production code for the AC(s) you own; their tests go GREEN.
  3. The other tests stay RED, visibly, until their owning tasks land.

The RED tests are the audit trail saying "this is a real commitment; the workspace knows it's not honoured yet." Their existence is load-bearing even before any production code is written for them.

## Pattern 2: deferring scope ACs to E2E

You look at the Scope ACs on the Spec (e.g. *'A manager can hide a stale AC from the active view without losing the audit trail'*) and you think "that's a UI thing, it'll be tested by E2E once the React surfaces land."

**Often wrong.** Scope ACs frame outcome contracts, but the contract usually decomposes into integration-level behaviours that can be asserted at the service layer right now. The example above is testable today: write a test that creates an AC, calls archive on it, queries the active list, asserts the AC isn't there but the row + its parent links still are.

E2E tests have a place — slow, brittle, only for the cross-surface stitching that integration tests can't reach. Defer to E2E only when the contract genuinely requires the browser. Otherwise lift the scope AC into a service-level integration test today.

A helpful check: read each scope AC and ask "can I assert this without a browser?" If yes, the test belongs at the service level. If you find yourself defaulting all scope ACs to E2E, you're skipping work the workspace can verify cheaply right now.

## Pattern 3: silently no-emitting tests

You write a test for an AC. You forget to call `tagAc('<canonical-ref>')` inside the test body. The test passes. You move on.

**Memex sees nothing.** A passing test that didn't tag is, to the workspace, a test that doesn't exist. `list_acs` still shows the AC at 0 tests. Coverage doesn't move.

The fix is to read `get_information(topic='ac-emission')` and make tagging part of the muscle memory of writing the test, not something you bolt on after. Every test you write that asserts an AC should start with `tagAc('<ref>')` before the assertion. If `list_acs` shows your AC still at 0 tests after a test run, the tag is missing or the helper isn't wired — look at the run output for `[ac-emit]` warnings.

## The rule

If you've moved a Spec to `build`, every active AC on it should have at least one tagged test in the codebase. The tests will be RED at first; that's correct. They go GREEN as their owning tasks land. You should never reach `verify` with active ACs at 0 tests.

If you DO see active ACs at 0 tests when calling `list_acs`, the answer is to write the RED tests now — not to declare 'wait for E2E' or 'wait for task t-N' or 'wait for the next agent'. The workspace's coverage signal is only as honest as the tests you write.
